agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation is a critical lifecycle operation in logistics and courier systems. A product manager has requested that users be able to cancel their orders, but the requirement lacks specificity on cancellation windows, eligible order states, refund policies, partial cancellation rules, and downstream effects on dispatch and fulfillment operations.","bounded_context":"Order Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request for pickup and delivery of a package, progressing through a defined lifecycle of states from placement to completion."},{"term":"Order Cancellation","definition":"A customer-initiated action that terminates an active order before final delivery, triggering state transition and potential refund processing."},{"term":"Order State","definition":"The current lifecycle phase of an order (e.g., Placed, Confirmed, Dispatched, In Transit, Delivered, Cancelled)."},{"term":"Cancellation Window","definition":"The period or set of order states during which cancellation is permitted."},{"term":"Refund","definition":"The return of payment to the customer following a successful cancellation, subject to refund policy rules."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The assignment of a courier to fulfill an order, marking the transition from warehouse/merchant processing to active delivery."},{"term":"Cancellation Fee","definition":"A potential charge applied when cancellation occurs after a cutoff point, compensating for costs already incurred."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can only be cancelled if it is in a cancellable state. Once an order reaches a terminal state (Delivered, Already Cancelled), cancellation is impossible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Allowing cancellation of a delivered or already-cancelled order would corrupt order state integrity and create accounting inconsistencies.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in state Delivered or Cancelled, when the user requests cancellation, then the system must reject the request with a clear error."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Only the customer who placed the order (the order owner) may initiate a cancellation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Allowing arbitrary users to cancel orders they do not own violates data ownership and could be exploited maliciously.","testable_assertion":"Given a cancellation request from user B for an order owned by user A, the system must reject the request with an authorization error."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Cancellation must trigger a refund according to the applicable refund policy for the order's current state.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Refund percentages and policies are business decisions that can change over time. The requirement to process some form of refund acknowledgment is firm, but the amount is policy-driven.","testable_assertion":"Given a successful cancellation, then a refund record is created with an amount determined by the refund policy applicable to the order's state at the time of cancellation."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"If a courier has already been dispatched, the system must notify the courier of the cancellation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Failing to notify a dispatched courier results in wasted operational effort, fuel costs, and a courier arriving at a destination for a cancelled order — a physical-world constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in Dispatched or In Transit state that is cancelled, then a cancellation notification event is emitted to the assigned courier."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A cancelled order must record the cancellation reason, timestamp, and initiator for audit purposes.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Audit trail integrity is essential for dispute resolution, fraud detection, and operational reporting. Without it, the system cannot reconstruct what happened.","testable_assertion":"Given a successful cancellation, the order record must contain a non-null cancellation_reason, cancelled_at timestamp, and cancelled_by user ID."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Cancellation is idempotent — requesting cancellation of an already-cancelled order must return success (not error) with the original cancellation details.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a design policy choice for API ergonomics, not a physical or data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an order already in Cancelled state owned by the requesting user, when cancellation is requested again, then the system returns HTTP 200 with the existing cancellation details."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order in state Placed or Confirmed owned by the requesting user","when":"The user requests cancellation with a valid reason","then":"The order transitions to Cancelled state, a full refund is initiated, an audit record is created, and the user receives confirmation of cancellation."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order in state Dispatched (courier assigned but not yet picked up)","when":"The user requests cancellation","then":"The order transitions to Cancelled state, the assigned courier is notified, a refund is initiated per the applicable refund policy, and an audit record is created."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order in state In Transit (courier has picked up the package)","when":"The user requests cancellation","then":"The system either rejects the cancellation with a clear message explaining why, or permits it with a cancellation fee applied — depending on the cancellation policy defined for this state."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An order in state Delivered","when":"The user requests cancellation","then":"The system rejects the request with a clear error message indicating the order has already been delivered and cannot be cancelled. The user is directed to the returns process."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"An order in state Cancelled","when":"The same user requests cancellation again","then":"The system returns a success response with the original cancellation details (idempotent behavior). No duplicate refund is created."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A user who does not own the order","when":"They attempt to cancel the order","then":"The system returns an authorization error and the order state is unchanged."},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A cancellation request missing a cancellation reason","when":"The request is submitted","then":"The system rejects the request with a validation error requiring a reason."},{"id":"AC-8","given":"Two concurrent cancellation requests for the same order","when":"Both are processed simultaneously","then":"Exactly one cancellation succeeds. The order transitions to Cancelled exactly once, and exactly one refund is initiated. The second request receives the idempotent success response."},{"id":"AC-9","given":"An order in a cancellable state","when":"The user cancels and the refund service is temporarily unavailable","then":"The order transitions to Cancelled, a refund-pending record is created, and the refund is retried asynchronously. The user is informed that cancellation succeeded and refund is being processed."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Concurrent cancellation and dispatch — user cancels while the system is simultaneously dispatching a courier","expected_behavior":"The system must handle this race condition atomically. Either the cancellation wins (courier is immediately recalled/notified) or the dispatch wins (user is informed the order has moved to Dispatched state and must re-attempt cancellation under that state's policy)."},{"scenario":"Partial order cancellation — user wants to cancel one item from a multi-item order","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous in current spec. If supported, the order remains active with reduced items and a partial refund. If not supported, the user must cancel the entire order. This needs PM clarification."},{"scenario":"Payment capture pending — order placed but payment not yet captured","expected_behavior":"Cancellation should void the payment authorization rather than initiating a refund, avoiding unnecessary charge-then-refund cycles."},{"scenario":"Repeated cancellation abuse — a user places and cancels orders at high frequency","expected_behavior":"The system should enforce rate limits or flag the account for review. Specific thresholds are a business policy decision."},{"scenario":"Order in a transitional state — e.g., being handed from one courier to another during a relay","expected_behavior":"The system must resolve the current effective state before processing cancellation. Transitional or intermediate states should either block cancellation with a retry-later message or map to the nearest canonical state."},{"scenario":"Cancellation after promotional credit applied — order used a coupon or loyalty points","expected_behavior":"Refund policy must account for promotional value. Credits/points should be restored to the user's account, and coupon reusability rules must be enforced (single-use coupons may need to be re-enabled)."},{"scenario":"Offline or degraded mode — user requests cancellation but backend services are partially down","expected_behavior":"Cancellation request should be queued with guaranteed delivery semantics. The user receives an acknowledgment that the request is being processed, with eventual consistency to final Cancelled state."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Order cancellation directly impacts revenue (lost sales), refund processing costs, and potential cancellation fee revenue. A poorly designed cancellation flow increases payment disputes and chargeback rates. A well-designed flow with clear policies reduces customer service costs.","operational":"Cancellation of dispatched orders wastes courier time and fuel. Timely courier notification is critical to operational efficiency. Audit trails reduce dispute resolution time. Idempotent design reduces support ticket volume from confused users.","user":"Cancellation capability is a baseline user expectation in modern commerce. Lack of self-service cancellation drives users to contact support (expensive) or initiate chargebacks (very expensive). Clear state-based messaging builds trust even when cancellation is denied.","risk":"Primary risks: (1) refund fraud through cancellation abuse, (2) race conditions causing double refunds or orphaned courier dispatches, (3) missing audit trails creating compliance exposure, (4) poor UX leading to chargebacks instead of cancellations.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Gain self-service cancellation capability, reducing frustration. Must receive clear feedback on cancellation eligibility and refund timelines."},{"group":"Couriers","impact":"Must receive timely cancellation notifications to avoid wasted trips. Late notifications erode courier trust and satisfaction."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Reduced ticket volume for cancellation requests. Need visibility into cancellation audit trail for dispute handling."},{"group":"Finance/Accounting","impact":"Must reconcile refunds accurately. Cancellation fees and partial refunds add complexity to revenue reporting."},{"group":"Fraud/Risk Team","impact":"Need monitoring for cancellation abuse patterns. Cancellation data feeds into fraud detection models."}]},"confidence":{"level":42,"reasoning":"The spec is a single sentence with no detail on cancellation windows, eligible states, refund policy, partial cancellation support, or downstream system integration. Nearly every concrete business rule required PM, finance, and operations input that was not provided. The acceptance criteria above represent the most common patterns in logistics order cancellation, but many branch points remain undefined.","high_confidence_areas":["Terminal states (Delivered, Cancelled) cannot be cancelled — universal invariant","Only order owner can cancel — fundamental authorization rule","Courier notification is required for dispatched orders — physical-world constraint","Audit trail is required — standard operational necessity"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact set of cancellable states and whether In Transit permits cancellation","Refund policy per state — full refund vs partial vs cancellation fee","Whether partial order cancellation is in scope","Cancellation reason — required or optional, free-text or enumerated","Time-based cancellation windows vs state-based windows","Promotional credit and coupon handling on cancellation"],"assumptions":["Order lifecycle includes at minimum: Placed, Confirmed, Dispatched, In Transit, Delivered, Cancelled","Cancellation is customer-initiated only (admin/system cancellation is a separate feature)","A refund mechanism exists in the system","Courier notification infrastructure exists"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec 'Users should be able to cancel their order' does not define which order states permit cancellation. Is cancellation allowed after dispatch? After pickup (In Transit)? The business impact varies enormously — allowing In Transit cancellation requires courier recall logistics; denying it simplifies operations but frustrates users.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Operations"],"options":["Allow cancellation only before dispatch (Placed, Confirmed)","Allow cancellation up to and including Dispatched, with courier notification","Allow cancellation at any pre-delivery state including In Transit, with escalating fees"],"recommendation":"Option B (allow through Dispatched) as the initial release — it covers the majority of cancellation requests while avoiding the complexity of recalling in-transit couriers. In Transit cancellation can be added in a follow-up with proper courier recall workflows."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Refund policy is entirely unspecified. Should cancelled orders always receive a full refund? Should there be a cancellation fee after certain states? What about orders where the courier has already incurred costs?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Finance/Accounting","Fraud/Risk Team"],"options":["Full refund regardless of state","Full refund before dispatch, partial refund (minus cancellation fee) after dispatch","Sliding scale based on fulfillment progress"],"recommendation":"Full refund before dispatch, policy-configurable fee after dispatch. This balances customer satisfaction with operational cost recovery and can be tuned without code changes."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Partial order cancellation (cancelling individual items within a multi-item order) is not addressed. This is a fundamentally different feature from whole-order cancellation with significant domain complexity.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Finance/Accounting"],"options":["Whole-order cancellation only for V1","Support partial cancellation from the start"],"recommendation":"Whole-order cancellation only for V1. Partial cancellation introduces item-level refund calculations, package re-weighing, and courier load adjustments that warrant a separate feature spec."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation is a critical lifecycle operation in logistics and courier systems. A product manager has requested that users be able to cancel their orders, but the requirement lacks specificity on cancellation windows, eligible order states, refund policies, partial cancellation rules, and downstream effects on dispatch and fulfillment operations.","bounded_context":"Order Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's

content.thinking:
The PM says "Users should be able to cancel their order." This is vague - no details about order states, time windows, refund policies, partial cancellations, etc. This is ambiguity - the requirement can be interpreted multiple ways.

Let me produce a thorough domain analysis.
